Aber auch die nachfolgenden Tracks machen eine gute Figur: "Wish" legt gleich noch mal einige Tonnen mehr an Düsterschwere auf und verbreitet eine noch apokalyptischere Stimmung als sein Vorgänger - wenn auch nicht ganz so melodisch ausgearbeitet. Und "Of Sorrow Blue" präsentiert sich abschließend als eine Art Mischung aus den ersten beiden: Die klassisch-harmonischen Elemente und Einschübe rücken wieder etwas mehr ins Zentrum, wobei anfangs die "schwerere Schwere" noch beibehalten wird und im Verlauf des Stücks einer mit Doublebass-Auftritt gekoppelten Tempoerhöhung weicht, bis man schließlich den Song mit viel akustischer Gitarre und Geige sanft ausklingen lässt. Hach, wie schön...

Im Anbetracht des hohen musikalischen Niveaus dieser EP - deren einziges Manko darin liegt, dass der Sound etwas fetter und weniger höhenlastig hätte ausfallen können - bin ich schon ganz heiß darauf zu hören, ob der angekündigte Longplayer im kommenden Jahr das halten kann, was "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" verspricht. Wenn ja, dann steht uns Großes ins Haus... Ansonsten: Leute, für die sich eine Mischung aus MY DYING BRIDE und GRAVEWORM gut anhört, sollten sich diese Scheibe unbedingt zu Gemüte führen, genauso wie Liebhaber epischem Düstermetalls.
Anspieltipps: im Prinzip alle drei Songs, aber ganz speziell "Eternal Autumn".

item.author: MK
item.p: Verdammt, wann zuletzt haben mich nur drei Songs einer Band derart mitgerissen, wie die der Schweden Forest of Shadows? "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" ist ein monumentales und epochales Epos, das die Schwermut My Dying Bride´s mit den Melodien der frühen Katatonia verbindet und nicht weniger als ein unvergängliches Meisterwerk darstellt. Hier existiert sie noch, die unvergleichliche Tiefe, die Seele in der Musik, die mit jeder Silbe und jeder Note ihr Innerstes nach aussen kehrt und eine Gänsehaut nach der anderen erzeugt; die Schwermut, die der herbstlichen Jahreszeit so eigen ist und selten treffender besungen wurde, als in "Wish": "Awakened this dying season, such a beauty to behold, oh autumn hath arrived, a tragedy painted dead..."; das Bewusstsein, das uns befällt, wenn man den Eindruck gewinnt, das hier Künstler am Werk sind, die von ihrem eigenen Werk selbst ergriffen sind. Anfang nächsten Jahres erscheint das erste komplette Album der Band, gerade rechtzeitig, um möglichen Entzugserscheinungen vorzubeugen. Diese EP, die übrigens zum Maxi-Preis erhältlich ist, ist der Hammer!!!!

item.author: Janick
item.p: "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" heißt das erste Lebenszeichen von Forest Of Shadows. Die MCD enthält drei Songs; das ist nicht viel werdet ihr denken, doch diese drei Songs schaffen es auf eine Spielzeit von knapp 30 Minuten! Die Songs haben mich sofort in ihren Bann gezogen. Die beiden Schweden schaffen es eine wunderschöne düstere und melancholische Atmosphäre zu erzeugen. Das Tempo ist gedrosselt und verstärkt somit diesen Eindruck und lässt das Ganze zu einem epischen Sound verschmelzen. Die Jungs erinnern mich an My Dying Bride und an alte Paradise Lost zu seeligen Gothic-Zeiten. Die Stimme im cleanen Bereich dagegen erinnert mich manchmal an das warme und wohlig tieftönende Organ eines Johan Edlund von Tiamat, besonders beim ersten Song "Eternal Autumn". Genau wie es dieser Titel verrät kredenzen uns Forest Of Shadows mit ihrer Musik den idealen Soundtrack zu unserer herbstlichen Jahreszeit. Die Jungs machen definitiv Lust auf mehr und ich freue mich schon jetzt auf ihr erstes Full Length Album, welches nächstes Jahr erscheinen soll. Fazit: Wer mit Bands wie Katatonia, My Dying Bride, alte Paradise Lost und Godgory etwas anfangen kann, sollte diese Jungs auf jeden Fall mal antesten. Ein weiterer Grund den beiden Schweden eine Chance zu geben besteht darin, daß diese MCD im Rahmen der Newcomer Kampagne von Rage Of Achilles Records zum Single Preis in die Läden kommt!

item.author: Sebastian
item.p: Nachdem mir FOREST OF SHADOWS schon auf dem Rage of Achilles-Label Sampler aufgefallen sind - das Review könnt Ihr HIER lesen - und ich sie da zur besten Band gekürt hatte, freue ich mich umso mehr das "Where dreams come true" nun in meinen Händen gelandet ist. Musikalisch tendeln die Schweden zwischen My Dying Bride und In the Woods .... , bieten aber sehr eigenständigen Dark- und Doom-Metal. Dieses Album, welches zum Preis einer Single in den Läden stehen wird, enthält die drei Songs "Eternal Autumn", "Wish" und "Of Sorrow Blue". Falls Ihr Euch denkt, dass drei Songs doch etwas wenig sind, muss man natürlich dazu sagen, dass eine Spielzeit von rund 30 Minuten erreicht wird, was ja schon an ein normales Album von Deceide rankommt. Für alle Freunde der düsteren, langsamen und anspruchsvollen Musik sind FOREST OF SHADOWS ein echter Kauftipp. Alle anderen dürfen sich gerne mal in ferne Wälder entführen lassen.

Episch, hymnisch, verträumt und irgendwie... schön. So in etwa könnte man die Klangwelten des Schattenwaldes mit ein paar Worten umschreiben. Worte die natürlich mit keiner Silbe die warme Tiefe dieser Kompositionen wiederspiegeln. Ein eben solches Wort ist auch das von der Band offiziell gewählte Banner "Darkmetal" welches mitnichten die Vielschichtigkeit des Songmateriales wiedergeben könnte (aber welcher Schubladendeckel könnte das von sich behaupten). Mit "Where dreams turn into dust" hat das Duo den perfekten Soundtrack zur Entspannung einer malträtierten Metal - Seele erschaffen. Ein Traum, welcher einerseits aus dezentem Flötenspiel (u.a. Intro), Akustikgitarren und vollen, sehr warmen, cleanen Gesangpassagen besteht, anderseits aber auch mit hymnenhaften E-Gitarrenleads, Growls und Screams kokettieren, ohne jemals das harmonisch ausbalancierte Gesamtbild zu stören oder gar zu zerstören. Nach 30 Minuten und 3 Songs (!!!) ist dann leider der im Titel beschriebene Punkt erreicht und der Traum löst sich in Wohlgefallen auf...was bleibt sind 4 wunderschöne Punkte... (taaataaa...)

item.author: Andreas
item.p: Was wird eine Band mit solchem Namen wohl für Musik spielen? Düsteren Metal, ganz recht. Reflexartig sucht man beim Flöten-Intro die Auswurftaste, in der Befürchtung, hier die Ethno-Vollbedienung zu bekommen, doch ist die Einleitung überstanden, geht es durchaus solide weiter. Forest Of Shadows bewegen sich ziemlich genau zwischen den Eckpfeilern My Dying Bride, ältere Theatre Of Tragedy, Amorphis und Moonspell. Alles sehr doomig, bedrückend und episch. Sie haben schöne Harmonien in den Stücken und insgesamt bleibt das Songwriting - trotzdem es gerne mal ausufert - einigermaßen nachvollziehbar. Ganz sicher nicht der Innovation letzter Schluss, aber allemal passend zur Jahreszeit und für Freunde des Genres sicher eine Entdeckung. Die Veröffentlichung ist Teil der Rage Of Achilles Newcomer-Serie und wird zum Preis einer Single in den Handel kommen.

item.author: Andreas
item.p: Dies ist das erste Lebenszeichen der schwedischen Dark Metaller und als Vorgriff auf das in Kürze erscheinende Album zu sehen. Nordische Folklore trifft auf britischen Düster Metal in der Art von My Dying Bride oder Katatonia. Zwischen geflüsterten Growls und cleanem Gesang überzeugt die Musik mit ihrer atmosphärischen Ausrichtung. Während die Gitarren bleischwer in doomiger Weise einem Lava Strom gleichen, sorgt die mittelalterliche Instrumentierung mit Flute usw. für pure Melancholie. Tiefdepressiv mit Hang zum theatralischen sind drei episch, bombastische Stücke entstanden, welche auch den kleinsten Lichtschein in ein dunkles Etwas verwandeln. Orchestraler Doom erstreckt sich über dreißig Minuten und zieht den Hörer tief in den Abgrund der menschlichen Seele. Die perfekte Begleitmusik für einen nächtlichen Friedhofsspaziergang für Hartgesottene. Während die cleanen Parts von einer morbiden Romantik umgeben sind, erleben die growligen Parts die Auferstehung des Bösen. Ein musikalischer Alptraum ohne Hoffnung.

item.author: Markus Eck
item.p: Dieses todtraurig anmutende schwedische Dunkelduo erfreut bzw. besser gesagt betrauert unsere Ohren mit ihrem Debütminialbum "Where Dreams Turn To Dust". Melancholisch getragen, episch machtvoll, bombastisch klangvoll, immens entrückt und in erster Linie zeitlos schwermütig zelebrieren die beiden dauerdepressiven Peinpoeten ihren massiv weltschmerzgetränkten Dark Metal, der mit einigen besinnlichen Akustikparts höchst sensibler und tränenerfüllter Emotionsnatur eindrucksvoll angereichert wurde. "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" weiß während knapp halbstündiger Spielzeit mit landestypischer, gewohnter skandinavischer Spielfinesse zu beeindrucken, jedoch ohne bereits vorhandene erfolgreiche Stilmuster solcher hier zu hörenden Inspirationshelfer wie älteren Katatonia oder frühen My Dying Bride blindlings zu kopieren. Forest Of Shadows setzen vielmehr auf eine prägnant nachdenklich wirkende Individualklangerscheinung, welche sich in wirklich faszinierender Instrumentaldarbietung veräußert: Von der mittelalterlich angehauchten, apokalyptischen Klangschwere einer Ausnahmeband wie Neurosis bis hin zur atmosphärischen Dramaturgiedichte nordischer und britischer Dark Metal-Outfits reicht die reflektierte Bandbreite der verarbeiteten Einflüsse. Forest Of Shadows packen sich die allerbesten Gestaltungselemente solcher Acts und arrangieren sie ideenreich und versiert zu einem hochepischen und nachhaltig einnehmenden Tragiksound, der unweigerlich in seinen hypnotisierenden Bann zieht. Zu Anfang des Jahres 2003 ist der Release des erstes Longplayers geplant - wenn die Band auf diesem die durch dieses tolle Minialbum geweckten Erwartungen erfüllen kann, steht ein weiteres grandioses Werk nächtlicher schmerzerfüllter Sinnessehnsüchte an.

Not one for your cheery, dreaming of California Fu-Manchu fans this one - more of an opus to the misery of dark woods, moon-lit nights and the beauty in loneliness. Echoing the melancholic atmospheres of past masters such as My Dying Bride, or classic Paradise Lost, this half hour cut of doomed insanity peers through to the modern world courtesy of that bastion of quality Rage of Achilles. Hinting at the glory days of early 90s British deathly doom, FoS are clear lovers of the classic traditions of funeral marches, despair and ruin. If you need another dose of down-tuned, down-trodden, and most definitely down-tempo then look no further, for this one should be right down your (dark and scary) alley.

item.author: Pedro Azevedo
item.p: The debut EP by Sweden's Forest of Shadows contains three tracks of melodic doom/death lasting a total of over half an hour. Slow and mournful, Forest of Shadows combine death and clean vox, and also add an array of other instruments for a mildly orchestral feel. The music isn't overly complex, yet contains enough variety and changes frequently enough to avoid any monotony if you enjoy this kind of doom metal. Simultaneously funereal and catchy enough, the music contains a strong emotional charge as well as a pleasant kind of unpretentious quality. Forest of Shadows manage to successfully gather influences from several of the European doom metal giants and use them without coming across as very derivative, especially for a debuting band. An impressive debut by these northern doomsters, and a smart signing by Rage of Achilles.

Three monstrously lengthy tracks of punishing, depressing doom from this two-man act out of Sweden. With likely influences obvious (My Dying Bride, early Paradise Lost, early Anathema, perhaps some early Katatonia), this is still strong material that doesn't have a completely unoriginal sound. The only thing I find distracting at times are the singing vocals, though when leaning more towards singing than speaking and held low in the mix they can be very nice. The aggressive vocals, on the other hand, are wonderful, resting off in the distance beneath a light sheen of reverb that makes them that much more interesting. The songwriting achieves an excellent balance between moving melodies and pounding heaviness, as well as spacious clean breaks and fluid changes. Synths are used incredibly tastefully, light lead lines accentuate devastating, heavy rhythms, the occasional acoustic break or use of upright bass adds further dimension to their sound, etc.

From a recording standpoint this is pretty damn good considering two people take care of all of the vocals and instrumentation involved (including live drums and keyboards). The heavier portions are slightly muddy, without a whole lot of distinction between guitar and bass, but the raw density works well with the cold, distant vocals, so that doesn't bother me. During the calmer moments things become a bit brighter and retain more clarity, further offsetting the changes, but not to the point where it becomes problematic. it may even be a strength.

Of course the fragmented lyrical content is full of utter despair and hopelessness, "How I wish in the midst of falling snow in a garden of endless grief my forlorn soul of misery where peace and rest can never dwell hope never comes I see no point in going on." A bit wordy and overdone, but anything less would be inappropriate for such musical ventures. The layout is very basic, mostly black with a simple band logo and some basic autumnal textures used very sparsely. Overall this is an astoundingly promising debut, and one of the most professional pieces of work I've ever heard from a project consisting of only two members. Definitely recommended.

item.author: Kristoffer R
item.p: Forest of Shadows härstammar från Västergötland. De startade som ett solo-projekt av Niclas Frohagen (Ningizzia, Shubend) men har senare utvecklats till ett fyra-manna band. De har nyligen fått kontrakt med engelska bolaget Rage of Achilles. Stilen varierar mycket från slö döds med aktustiska delar till väldigt mörk, melankolisk metal. Brutala avgrundsvrål varvas med vanlig sång(inge jävla falsett sång).

Eftersom att det är MCD så innehåller den bara tre stycken låtar, men ändock en speltid av 29:14 min. Skivan första spår, "Eternal Autumn", inleds med ett oerhört vackert flöjtstycke spelat av Anna Ekblad, för att senare gå över till tunga gitarriff o avgrundsvrål. Det andra spåret, "Wish", har ingen direkt inledning utan börjar med tunga gitarriff o Niclas fruktansvärt bra dödsång. Även det sista spåret, "Of Sorrow Blue", börjar utan inledning men här sjunger han med vanlig sång till en början, för att sedan börja med dödsången. Låten avslutas med ett akustiskt stycke som är välidgt sorgset o vackert.
Den här mcd:n är bland det bästa jag hört. Skivan har en sån jävla känsla o stämning. De har planerat att släppa en fullängdare någongång under 2002, och jag ser fram emot den som fan!

item.author: Stephen H
item.p: Opening with the gentle sound of a flute being played, this 3 track mini album has more than just a passing resemblance to My Dying Bride. The flute soon flows effortlessly into a somber guitar led piece with growled vocals, before Forest of Shadows go all Katatonia on us with carefully picked guitars and nicely sung vocals, and then resorting back to the doomy vision they had created at the beginning.

This then lays down the format for the rest of the mini album, a mixture a growled and clean vocals with the band forging an epic sounding doom framework, heartfelt feeling permeating every minute and although the sound they produce isn't dissimilar to the bands I've mentioned, there's a distinct freshness to the whole proceedings. It's slightly unfair however to base the sound of Forest Of Shadows on those bands but the sound they produce isn't too far away from them and it's the closest comparison.

Guitars weave along a meandering pathway whilst the vocalist growls away in the background, and on more than one occasion, flashes of genius show themselves in the song structures. Bringing to mind the feeling of epic that this style of doom can often deliver, Forest of Shadows are clearly a band who know a good tune when they hear it. Mid paced track 'Wish' has all the ingredients of this, often slow but never boring, the guitar weaves in and out of a mixture of growled and cleanly sung vocal lines, producing a track which immediately demands your attention. Final track, 'Of Sorrow Blue', takes that remit even further with a faster (but still only mid paced) doom epic which has some inspired passages of surging guitars and beautifully growled vocals, interspersed with slower moments with more of a chugging riff, flutes (or possibly violins) and acoustic guitars appear later which only reinforce the overall vast sound of the track. The appearance of double bass towards the end combined with the growled vocals just reminds the listener that the band have all their bases covered and as the orchestral feel makes another not unwelcome appearance, I'm already ready to press the replay button. Unfortunately it's all over too quickly, a mere 30 minutes but this only wets the appetite for the upcoming full length.
If My Dying Bride, Katatonia or even earlier Paradise Lost floats your boat then this EP will be a most welcome addition to the collection.

item.author: Marcus Grahn
item.p: När MY DYING BRIDE-färgade FOREST OF SHADOWS knåpar ihop finurliga melodier - som i inledande "Eternal Autumn" - är de riktigt bra. När göteborgarna istället gör det lätt för sig och på tomgång harvar segdragna doomklyschor låter det därefter.

Debuten består endast av tre skapelser, men är sammanlagt nästan en halvtimme lång. Vokalisten är katastrofalt kass när han försöker sjunga rent, batteristen behöver variera sig och det är synnerligen lätt att hamna i tankebanor som berör annat än musiken när "Wish" och "Of Sorrow Blue" brummar ur högtalarna.
FOREST OF SHADOWS är (än så länge) ett dussinband med lätträknade höjdpunkter.

item.author: Gary Reeves
item.p: "Disturbing swedish doom metal that promises much"

Jesus Christ, where did these guys spring up from?! (erm..Sweden.) This mini cd bridges the gap until their full length platter is relesed next year and what a space filler!! Any individuals on the verge of suicide should avoid 'Where Dreams...' at all costs while those of sound mind should place their advance orders now. The three songs found on this disc are about as epic as serious doom metal can be. 'Eternal Autumn' is the death march into oblivion with echoing funeral chords, emotive classical segues and a downtuned undertow lurking beneath while 'Wish' rams this desperation and agony home further. The final hymn, 'Of Sorrow Blue' is a turbulent and disturbing journey. mercifully laid to rest by some mournful acoustics. Let's pray that Forest Of Shadows can maintain this grasp on the claustrophobic when they record their debut. If they do, it's time for My Dying Bride to vent concern.

item.author: Catherine Yates
item.p: Gothic sweeps of melancholic metal form the latest offering from London label Rage Of Achilles in the form of Swedish duo Forest of Shadows. For those black-clad souls who stare sorrowfully to the likes of Katatonia and My Dying Bride, this is gloom-or-be-gloomed tinged manna from heaven. However, those who were more taken with the bloodcurdling dirge frenzies of labelmates Goatsblood, will be heartened by the fact that these epic laments only have a total running time of 30 minutes.

item.author: James Hinchliff
item.p: It's very pleasing to see Forest of Shadows finally sign a record contract, since they've been producing extremely impressive work in demo form for some time. Indeed, 'Where Dreams Turn To Dust' was originally their third demo release, every aspect of it so professional that it's presented here with only slightly modified artwork and almost exactly as originally bedroom-recorded, not that you'd know it as the sombre and majestic, early Katatonia-esque doom lumbers out of the speakers with as much production punch as Unholy or My Dying Bride. Can't wait for the full-lengther due early next year.

item.author: Jack
item.p: "One of the best ever mcd/demo released in the history of metal."

When I say mcd/demo, what I mean to say that it was previously demo material that was on mp3.com, and now an intelligent label has finally decided to sign these champions of dark metal up, Rage of Achilles is the intelligent label, that should hopefully catapult this band into extreme metal limelight. And they thoroughly deserve. Forest of Shadows are comprised of two members: Micce Andersson (responsible for bass, guitar, programming, synthesizers and violin) and Niclas Frohagen (guitar, programming, synthesizers and vocals). Influencing these overly talented musicians are outfits such as Katatonia, Opeth, My Dying Bride and Anathema. All fantastic bands in their own rights. All radically different. What Forest of Shadows does is take the best elements of each band, for example the somber parts of My Dying Bride, the cool solos of Opeth, and the heartfelt lyrics and vocals of Katatonia. But don't make the mistake of thinking that Forest of Shadows rip any of these bands off. They are completely innovative and unique.

Niclas' voice is extremely hard to describe, and I wouldn't really do it justice to try and compare, or pigeonhole it with any other extreme act of today. Extremely deep, guttural roars that have a nice hollow sound to it. His clean voice is essentially indescribable, its unlike anything I've heard in metal today, suffice to say its fresh and compelling. "Where Dreams Turn to Dust" is comprised of three songs spanning nearly 30 minutes, that equates to epic songs in the tradition of Opeth and My Dying Bride. Never boring or drab each of the three tracks is unique in composition. All tracks are equally awesome and as a result there is no real standout track, only standout tracks. Bottom Line: Go to www.mp3.com, downloading a few Forest of Shadows, support this band by buying their upcoming mcd, and I guarantee they will change your perception of good, innovative metal. Forest of Shadows will go very far in future years.

album.name: where dreams turn to dust | demo 2000

item.author: Matthew
item.p: The lone word that springs to mind after hearing this band is Wow! Hailing from Gothenburg, Sweden, Forest of Shadows is a two piece band that deliver an unrivaled cornucopia of epic Doom, Gothic, and Power metal, all gathered into one bleak and emotionally devastating package.

Sounding like a head on collision between Opeth, old My Dying Bride and Katatonia, Forest Of Shadows take those popular styles and blend them ingeniously to cross over and appeal to fans of multiple dark music genres. Obviously their music has touched many as they have been reigning atop the Mp3 Gothic Metal charts for the past month and a half with their tracks "Under The Dying Sun" and "The Silent Cry" with three other tracks close on their tails.

Forest Of Shadows is a very complex and ambitious band, with multi-faceted songs often nearing or exceeding ten minutes in length. With multiple passages of acoustic guitar serenades, and haunting flute, piano, and violin solos, the band exhibit both a penchant for classical inspired doom with more upbeat, double-bass driven parts that gallop full-steam ahead into the realm of power metal.

But most importantly, the two musicians have a knack for composing striking melodies, as the twin guitars resonate with grief and sorrowful feeling, topped by agonized death metal growls and gorgeous clean vocal harmonies, best exemplified in an astounding melodic break in the song "Wish". With lush male choir vocals drifting atop a charging metallic backdrop, the band contributes an immortal moment in this genre that will be appreciated by many. It's a truly remarkable passage; long enough to captivate yet brief enough not to be monotonous.

Another memorable track is "Of Sorrow Blue", which is literally a maze of changes and shifts in mood, clocking in at over eleven minutes. A brooding tone is set immediately in the strong opening as a sludgy drum/power chord backbone is wed to a sweet, haunting riff. Gothic male vocals appear for the early verses as the melody carries to reach a short acoustic break, then to venture off into several different darker and heavier moments, where the guttural vocals take center stage. The song enfolds the listener within a suffocating mood that reaches its final destintation with a somber acoustic outro punctuated by a solo violin.

Forest Of Shadows are an amazing band with a wealth of talent, and their sound is so vast and encompassing in its grandiosity that it will not be hard for fans of dark progressive metal, goth metal, or death metal to fall head over heals in love with their music.

item.author: Jeremy GoreChrist Whitman
item.p: Near brilliant doom/death with violins, flutes, and tons of melody. Forest of Shadows also mixes, with taste I must add, some elements of "goth metal" for extra range. What makes their sound work is their dynamic songwriting approach, trading off single instrument sessions to dual harmony segments and then exploding with rather emotional riffs. The melodies that flow throughout the disc seem molded into perfection, and they give Where Dreams Turn to Dust the long-lasting re-play value that most doom/death albums lack. Equally impressive is the fact that all instruments, with the exception of the flute, are played by Forest of Shadows' two members Micce Anderson and Niclas Frohagen. The only real problem is with the clean vocals, which remain slightly off-key and distinct in spots; justifying this flaw, most death metal vocalists who attempt clean vocals are much worse. I personally hear a lot of Skepticism-isms, especially in "Wish", and Paradise Lost influence. Everything else is refreshingly original, and as if I haven't given Forest of Shadows enough credit, these guys definitely have the guts to experiment with major chords in a way that I have never heard within these genres. Flowing rhythms, drenched in sad minors, occasionally drift towards an uplifting major scale for mere seconds, and then the minors resurge. Beautiful. Buy this, and support these guys.

item.author: Aldo Q
item.p: Forest of Shadows never ceases to amaze me and with this demo they have grown to new heights. The excellently performed melodic death/doom metal they serve us on this demo, clearly shows that they should have been signed to a label a long time ago. While this demo finally gained them a record deal with the small label Molded Skull Records (who will also release this demo as a Mcd) it is still beyond me why this took so long and why it had to be such a small label. After a beautiful sorrow filled flute intro, heavier sounds roll in creating a truly melancholic atmosphere. Their solid and slow metal is complimented by the occasional use of a violin or acoustic brake. Filling almost 30 minutes with just 3 songs should give you a clear indication about the length of these songs. Forest of Shadows is a school example of the melodic death/doom genre. Of course this means they won't win any originality prices, but they pull it off so skilfully that this really doesn't matter. Where most bands try to stick out by experimenting, Forest of Shadows stick out by keeping to the often-threaded paths. Obvious references would be Katatonia, My Dying Bride and in my opinion also Empyrium (especially the occasional clean vocals). You can download the entire album from http://www.mp3.com/fos/ so I don't see what could stop you from checking out this demo!

item.author: Jesse H
item.p: It is extremely rare a band that is so talented and has such a strong cult following goes so long without a killer record deal. Infact, I simply cannot believe that the 'major' metal labels haven't jumped all over this incredible band. Even if someone wasn't convinced by the music (which is hard to believe), Forest of Shadows has completely dominated the mp3.com gothic metal charts for months. They have earned nearly $5000 simply by putting their music online. Trust me when I say their popularity is due to the quality of their music, not some intensive promotional campaign.

That said, I suppose there are still some of you out there who are still oblivious to this band, so hopefully this review will change that. "Where Dreams Turn to Dust" is Forest of Shadows' newest and strongest contribution to the genre of emotional melodic doom metal. The mCD contains three massive tracks which together span nearly 30 minutes of unbelievable depressing doom metal, with influences ranging from older Opeth and Katatonia to My Dying Bride. The first track, "Eternal Autumn" begins with the somber sound of a flute, which is performed by the band themselves, not a synth. It's not long before a wall of guitars collapse upon your unsuspecting ears and you are completely and utterly lost within an atmosphere of devastating sadness. Throughout your aural journey you will visit skillfully played acoustic passages, most similar to "Morningrise"-era Opeth and excellently executed clean and death style vocals -- not to mention brilliantly interwoven violins most similar to older My Dying Bride. One thing is clear, this band knows how to write extremely simple songs with the maximum emotional impact. Although I would have loved a real drummer on the recording, the slow and simple rhythms allow a drum machine to go virtually unnotice. The second song "Wish" is my personal favorite of the three, but none of them are weak at any point. There is no doubt in my mind that if you are a fan of Opeth, Katatonia, My Dying Bride, or any quality emotional metal band, that you will adore this mCD. Forest of Shadows is the real deal folks.

Supposedly this mCD will be released on a small label called Molded Skull Records sometime soon, but hopefully a label with some money will wake up and realize what is under their noses. My advice to everyone is to check out this band as soon as you can. I'm willing to bet in a couple years when this band is big "everyone" will be talking about how they were into this band years ago. For now the best way to check out Forest of Shadows is through their enormously successful mp3.com page at www.mp3.com/fos.

item.author: Pedro
item.p: Given the quality of songwriting and execution demonstrated by their previous two demos, I'm genuinely surprised that Forest of Shadows ever made it to a third demo without being enslaved by a nefarious record label. Well, this demo has to be their last. From the moment the opener "Eternal Autumn"'s first riff emerges from the speakers, after the gentle flute intro, it is totally obvious that the band have reached a level of musical skill and professionalism which not only matches that of their contemporaries, but in the cases of many of those peers exceeds it entirely; if the process which led to bands being plucked from their bedrooms and given a half-decent position in the metal hierarchy were not so apparently random, Forest of Shadows would have been entertaining many more people for some time.

Now, to be honest, I'm not saying that Where Dreams Turn Into Dust is perfect; there is still a Katatonia influence nudging the band's own personality slightly aside, and even within their field, Forest of Shadows have absolutely no chance of winning any awards for originality. Melodic doom/death metal has been done to, ahem, death by a long line of bands stretching back over a decade, and there's not much left for the genre to do in terms of revitalising itself; on reflection, though, Forest of Shadows make this work in their favour, because they are forced to rely on their aptitude for creating good music in their chosen pigeon-hole, rather than relying on any forced or spurious meanderings in the name of "experimentation". Consequently, since their soundworld is virtually pre- defined, the band have concentrated on perfecting the formula, and as such their output has all the ingredients one might expect, such as mournful clean singing supporting the throat-shredding roars, minimal but musically relevant and non-trivially orchestrated keyboards, acoustic interludes, violins etc. etc., all produced as well as any other player in this particular sub-genre. Without a doubt, Forest of Shadows are among the masters of their chosen field. Someone needs to discover them quickly.

item.author: Nikki Sixx
item.p: Forest Of Shadows, this is where you lose yourself at times of deep sorrow and everlasting solitude while listening to their music, in a misty and dark forest of shadows letting the forlorn shades speak of tales of bleakness. "Under the Dying Sun" is their second demo released during 2000 and it consists only of one composition, the 15-minute ode to melancholy "Under the Dying Sun." Katatonia and My Dying Bride influences can be heard here and there, but being well-hidden since Forest Of Shadows are only influenced and do not copy, playing music from the artist's heart straight to the listener's.

I could write down as a review only the lyrics of "Under the Dying Sun" that are pure poetry to my eyes, bleeding words, grieving tears from the eyes of Ligeia falling in the doleful darkness; they represent the sound of the band in a way that no man could do better. When the time comes to write down my thoughts concerning this band they flee from my mind, I cannot speak, I cannot recall what I was thinking simply because this band is so special to me dragging my soul out of my body drowning me into a forest of shadows.

."Under the Dying Sun" seeing its scarlet rays fading in the darkening horizon. The mournful guitar riffing along with the floating doleful ethereal keyboard melodies that lurk behind the guitar sound open the gates of bereavement and Niclas' almost-crying desperate grunts enter to complete the bleak soundscape. The mournful slow guitar riffing dances in a heart-rending way with the melancholic guitar chords as withered rose-leafs start falling around you welcoming the grieving violin melodies when you realize that there's no escape from this bitter nightmare where pain and sorrow seem like joyful memories in front of the burden you have to bear. Niclas' grunts, desperate screams, melancholic chants and whispers are utterly descriptive as if he was an actor in the most tear-filled drama living every single word he utters from his bitter lips, words of the heart scarring deep your fragile soul becoming one with the melodies. The bleakness that the lyrics unfold harmonize in painful ways with the bleeding musical background, lyrics that speak of your beloved one embracing eternal sleep, fading "in fields of green, under the dying sun," "breathing her final breath," "in the fields of green you see her die," "you cry out in grief, for your beloved one," "you see her die."
"Under the Dying Sun" will drown you in 15 minutes of utterly emotional melodic doom/death metal that will last for all eternity; don't miss it.

item.author: Pedro
item.p: This is their newer promotional CD, weighing in at a heftier fifteen minutes, yet only featuring one song. That snippet of information alone would have many people screaming and trying to hide, but if you're anything like me, you'll think that long songs are great. "Under the Dying Sun" is generally noticably superior to the "The Silent Cry", with greater depth to the songwriting, and some proper metal sections, with grinding, sludgy guitar riffs.

There's a very occasional descent into twee melodies in the keyboard parts, which is slightly niggling, but ephemeral enough to not detract too much from the otherwise omnipresent melancholy. All too often, when essaying long songs, bands fall into the trap of joining several short songs together, with stilted and artificial results, but Forest of Shadows pleasantly seem to have composed "Under the Dying Sun" in a more holistic fashion. This apparent consideration of the listener creates a musical experience which is kept interesting, yet also slow and dirge-like, and wins my approval.

Again, the sound quality is very high indeed, as befits the genre, and on the strength of this, I have to assume strong label interest is just around the corner. Now, if they can just do something about that dodgy band name, this lot could well be onto something...

item.author: Pedro
item.p: Forest of Shadows come from Gothenburg, and so, as you might expect, they play..... doom?!? Yes, ignoring the hordes of widdly metal bands, this lot spew forth some pretty heavy doom metal. A quick check of my Children's Colour Atlas (1987 edition, which still has East Germany on it) confirmed that Göteborg is pretty much halfway between Yorkshire and Finland - a description which applies equally well to their music. The Silent Cry is their promo CD from last year, containing two songs, the original title track and a Katatonia cover. The original song is very nice indeed; violins meld with crushingly heavy guitars and transport the listener across time and space to Yorkshire in about 1991, yet the Finnish school of doom exemplified by the likes of Unholy (slow, with a crushing bassy sound) is also called to mind. It's startling just how well Forest of Shadows have captured the vibe of that particular period of metal history (the heyday of the likes of Paradise Lost and My Dying Bride), yet also managed to sound contemporary. Subtle and not overused keyboards embroider the music without distracting, adding kitsch or lightening the mood, and the rest of the band produce a well-executed blend of melody and aggressiveness which doesn't descend into noodling. Topped with a deep-voiced and gruff growling singer, the band create a dark and gloomy atmosphere in a traditional style. The cover is "Rainroom" from Brave Murder Day, and is pretty straight, and so reasonably superfluous, although credit is due for the convincing Åkerfeldt impression, and it does serve to highlight another reference point for the band's own work. No expense has been spared on the studio, either, as both songs have top-notch sound quality, and as I have this on CD-R, comes across sounding better than half the albums I have by signed bands. Overall, this is a fine production, and fans of sonically detailed doom metal should keep an eye out for this lot.

item.author: Aldo Q
item.p: Forest of Shadows is a doom-metal band who are heavily inspired by early work of My Dying Bride, Katatonia and Anathema. On this demo (pressed on CD), the band still exists out of one person, Niclas (also a member of Ningizzia). He does all instruments and vocals himself. A pity is that the production is not too good. The band has grown after the release of this demo, (including the use of violins) but you can't hear that on this demo yet. The music is slow and heavy, just like death-doom should be. Most noticeable are the grunts; they are very low, almost whispered grunts. This apparently because else the neighbours complained about the noise. While not overly creative, this demo did impress me and I do think this band, certainly with the addition the extra band member now, can go a long way (I'll admit I cheated as I have already heard a new song by them and it sounds very good!).
